title: Entropic Reciprocity in Time-Reversed Young Interferometry
abstract: We show that time-reversed Young interferometry reorganizes, rather than reverses, optical entropy. A fixed detector conditions the reciprocal source--detector Green function and produces a source-label probability distribution. Marginal entropies in the standard and time-reversed geometries are generally unequal; the reciprocal invariant is instead the mutual information between source and detector coordinates. Near a destructive response, the conditioned source-label entropy can decrease while Fisher information for small phase, tilt, or defocus perturbations increases. The result identifies time-reversed Young interferometry as a source-space information processor with no analogue in ordinary detector-plane fringe readout.
summary: This paper shows that time-reversed Young interferometry acts as an information processor that redistributes rather than reverses entropy. It identifies mutual information as the fundamental invariant between standard and time-reversed optical readouts and demonstrates how operating near destructive interference can boost parameter sensitivity.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether time-reversed Young interferometry (TRY) reverses or reorganizes optical entropy and seeks to identify the reciprocal invariant between standard and time-reversed geometries.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that TRY reorganizes rather than reverses entropy, identifying mutual information as the true reciprocal invariant, and show that sensitivity to perturbations can be enhanced near destructive interference at the cost of detection probability.
method: The study uses information-theoretic tools including Shannon entropy, Mutual Information, and Fisher Information applied to a statistical framework of joint event distributions and Bayesian updates.
model_or_system: A reciprocal linear optical system modeled using scalar paraxial diffraction theory (Fresnel approximation) comparing standard Young interference (detector-plane readout) to a time-reversed geometry (source-plane readout).
key_observables: Detector-space entropy, conditioned source-label entropy, Mutual Information, Fisher Information for parameter estimation, and detection probability.
important_parameters: Physical perturbations (phase, tilt, defocus, etc.), residual null-depth (epsilon-bar), background noise floor (b), and the dimensionless reciprocal tuning parameter (beta).
main_assumptions: The model assumes scalar paraxial approximation, optical reciprocity, and the absence of nonreciprocal media or polarization-dependent effects.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a conceptual comparison of geometries; Figure 2 shows the relationship between detector entropy, source entropy, and mutual information; Figure 3 illustrates the trade-off between sensitivity enhancement and photon-budget cost in a regularized null model.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the physical geometries and the statistical framework, moves to establishing the entropic reciprocity and mutual information invariance, analyzes the enhancement of Fisher information in the null-response regime, and concludes with the trade-offs involving background noise and detection probability.
why_interesting: It provides a novel information-theoretic perspective on optical interferometry, specifically how 'source-space programming' can be used to engineer measurement sensitivity, which is highly relevant to quantum optics and precision parameter estimation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Conceptual comparison between standard Young readout and time-reversed Young readout. In the standard geometry, the detector screen measures the marginal distri- bution pstd θ (x) = R dy pθ(x, y). In the time-reversed Young geometry, a fixed detector at x0 selects the conditional source- label distribution pTRY θ (y|x0) = pθ(x0, y)/ R dy pθ(x0, y). Both readouts arise from the same reciprocal source–detector kernel, but they correspond to different statistical reduc- tions: marginalization in detector space versus conditioning in source space.

FIG. 2. Illustrative numerical evaluation of Eqs. (5)–(7) for a reciprocal two-path model as a dimensionless tuning pa- rameter β is varied. The detector entropy HX, conditioned source entropy HY |x0, and mutual information I(X; Y ) are computed from the same joint kernel but from different sta- tistical reductions. The important point is not equality of their numerical scales, but their inequivalent status: HX and HY |x0 are marginal or conditional entropies, whereas I(X; Y ) is the reciprocal source–detector information.

FIG. 3. Illustrative null-response tradeoff based on Eq. (13). As the residual null-depth control parameter ¯ϵ is reduced, the conditioned source entropy HY |x0 decreases, while the TRY Fisher information per detected event increases. The normal- ized detection probability Pdet/Pmax shows the complemen- tary photon-budget cost of operating near a dark response. Thus the null does not create unbounded information; it redis- tributes information into the conditional source-label record, with physical regularization by background, finite aperture, and normalization.
